docs.title: A High Throughput Two-Choice Assay for Drosophilids
docs.text: 🚀 Get Started 📖 User Guide 📚 Reference Thesis (PDF)

docs.title: What is 16FlYMaze?
docs.text:
16FlYMaze is a complete hardware-software platform for running automated optogenetic Two-Alternative Forced Choice (2AFC) behavioral experiments on Drosophila melanogaster and other drosophilids in 16 simultaneous Y-shaped arenas.

The system was developed in the Turner Lab at Janelia Research Campus (HHMI) to enable high-throughput, reproducible behavioral neuroscience experiments that combine:

  • Odor discrimination — two distinct odors delivered to the left and right arms of each Y-maze
  • Optogenetic reinforcement — precisely timed LED light pulses delivered as conditioned stimuli
  • Automated tracking — GPU-accelerated real-time detection of fly position and choice

CAD renders: arena stack (left) and full imaging rig with overhead FLIR camera (right)

docs.title: Feature Highlights
docs.text:
• 16 Simultaneous Arenas

    Run identical or independent experiments on 16 Y-shaped arenas at once, maximizing throughput and enabling within-session comparisons.

  • GPU-Accelerated Tracking

    Real-time fly detection and position tracking using NVIDIA CUDA (CuPy & CUCIM), processing all 16 arenas at camera frame rate.

  • Optogenetic Stimulation

    Programmable LED pulse patterns (pulse width, period, count, delay, intensity) delivered via serial Arduino interface.

  • Automated Odor Delivery

    Odor valve control over ROS2 / Raspberry Pi interface, with Alicat MFC flow rate management.

  • Full GUI Suite

    Seven dedicated PyQt5 applications — from hardware configuration and mask design to live experiment control and post-hoc analysis.

  • Multiple Paradigms

    Support for 2AFC, Deterministic Finite-State (DFSE), and Multi-Level (DMLE) experiment designs, enabling diverse behavioral tasks.

  • Email Notifications

    Gmail API integration for remote experiment monitoring — receive alerts, progress reports, and completion notifications.

  • Built-in Analysis

    Automated post-hoc data processing, trajectory analysis, and video generation tools included out of the box.

docs.title: Reporting Bugs
docs.text:
If you encounter a bug, please open an issue with:

  • A clear description of the problem
  • Steps to reproduce it
  • Your Python version and OS
  • Any relevant error messages or tracebacks

docs.title: Suggesting Features
docs.text:
Feature requests are welcome! Please open an issue with the enhancement label and describe:

  • The use case or motivation
  • A description of the proposed feature
  • Any relevant prior art or references

docs.title: Development Setup
docs.text:
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/neurorishika/16FlYMaze.git
cd 16FlYMaze

# Create a conda environment
conda create -n sixteeny-dev python=3.8
conda activate sixteeny-dev

# Install in editable mode with development dependencies
pip install -e .

docs.title: Code Style
docs.text:
• Follow PEP 8 for Python code style
  • Use descriptive variable names and docstrings
  • Keep functions focused on a single responsibility
  • Document all public classes and methods with docstrings matching the existing style

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
The documentation is built with MkDocs Material.

To build and preview locally:

pip install mkdocs-material mkdocs-minify-plugin
mkdocs serve

Then open http://127.0.0.1:8000 in your browser.

Documentation source files are in docs/. Please update relevant pages when making code changes.

docs.title: Pull Request Process
docs.text:
1. Fork the repository and create a feature branch
  2. Make your changes with clear, atomic commits
  3. Ensure existing functionality is not broken
  4. Update documentation as needed
  5. Open a pull request with a clear description of your changes

docs.title: Data Processor
docs.text:
Module: sixteeny.analysis.16Y_data_processor

A PyQt5 GUI application for post-hoc processing of 16FlYMaze experimental data.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Data Processor loads raw tracking arrays and experiment logs from completed experiment folders and applies cleaning, alignment, and metric computation to produce analysis-ready output files.

docs.title: Coordinate Realignment
docs.text:
def euclidean_distance(point1: np.ndarray, point2: np.ndarray) -> float
Compute Euclidean distance between two 2D points.

def realign_lr(
    start_arm: float,
    point: np.ndarray,
    arena_index: int
) -> np.ndarray
Rotate fly position coordinates into a canonical Left–Start–Right frame.

start_arm Rotation applied 0.0 +120° 1.0 0° (no rotation) 2.0 −120°

Clockwise arenas additionally mirror the x-axis (× [−1, 1]).

def realign_odor(
    odor_vector: np.ndarray,
    point: np.ndarray
) -> np.ndarray
Re-align fly position into an Odor 1–Start–Odor 2 frame by mirroring the x-axis when odors are swapped.

docs.title: Computed Metrics
docs.text: Metric Description Choice fraction Fraction of trials in which Odor 1 (or left) was chosen Response time Time from trial start to first arm entry Dwell time Time spent in the chosen arm before reward evaluation Reward rate Fraction of trials with reward delivered Trial length Total duration from trial start to next trial start

docs.title: Output Files
docs.text:
Processed data is written to CSV files in the experiment directory:

File Contents processed_data.csv Per-trial metrics for all arenas trajectories.npy Realigned trajectory arrays

docs.title: Camera Controller
docs.text:
Module: sixteeny.controller.camera

Controls the FLIR Spinnaker machine-vision camera used to image all 16 arenas.

docs.title: SpinnakerCamera
docs.text: A high-level wrapper around the PySpin SDK for acquiring frames from a FLIR camera.

docs.title: Constructor
docs.text:
SpinnakerCamera(
    camera_index=0,
    exposure_time=10000,
    gain=0,
    frame_rate=30,
    image_width=1920,
    image_height=1200,
    pixel_format="Mono8"
)
Parameter Type Description camera_index int Index of the camera in the Spinnaker camera list exposure_time float Exposure time in microseconds gain float Analog gain frame_rate float Target frame rate (fps) image_width int Image width in pixels image_height int Image height in pixels pixel_format str Pixel format (e.g., "Mono8")

docs.title: Methods
docs.text:
def init(self) -> None
Initialize the camera: configure acquisition parameters and begin acquisition.

def close(self) -> None
Stop acquisition and release the camera.

def get_frame(self) -> np.ndarray
Acquire and return a single frame as a NumPy array.

def __enter__(self) -> SpinnakerCamera
def __exit__(self, ...) -> None
Context manager support.

docs.title: Module-level Functions
docs.text:
def list_cameras() -> PySpin.CameraList
Return a list of all connected Spinnaker cameras. Initializes the PySpin System singleton if not already initialized.

def video_writer(save_queue: queue.Queue, writer) -> None
Worker function for asynchronous video writing. Reads frames from save_queue and writes them using a skvideo writer. Send None to stop.

docs.title: Usage Example
docs.text:
from sixteeny.controller.camera import SpinnakerCamera

with SpinnakerCamera(camera_index=0, exposure_time=5000, frame_rate=20) as cam:
    frame = cam.get_frame()
    # frame is a NumPy array (H × W) in Mono8 format
    print(frame.shape)

docs.text: Package Purpose PySpin FLIR Spinnaker Python API numpy Frame data arrays skvideo Video writing (requires FFMPEG) cupy GPU array support (optional)

docs.text:
Module: sixteeny.controller.led

Controls the custom Arduino-based LED controllers for optogenetic stimulation and IR backlight.

docs.title: Helper Functions
docs.text:
def hex_to_rgb_intensity(hex_value: bytes) -> tuple[int, int, int]
Convert a hex color string (e.g., b"#FF8000") to RGB intensity percentages (0–100).

docs.title: Class: LEDController
docs.text: Controls all 4 LED modules (16 arenas) via serial connections.

docs.title: Constructor
docs.text:
LEDController(
    ports=["COM3", "COM5", "COM6", "COM4"],
    baudrate=115200,
    arena_panel_ids=["0001", "0010", "1000", "0100"],
    irgb_scaling_factors=None   # (16, 4) array of scaling factors per arena per channel
)
Parameter Type Description ports list[str] COM port names for each of the 4 LED modules baudrate int Serial baud rate (default: 115,200) arena_panel_ids list[str] 4-character binary panel IDs irgb_scaling_factors np.ndarray (16, 4) scaling factors (IR, R, G, B) per arena

docs.title: Lifecycle
docs.text:
def setup_connections(self) -> None
Open serial connections to all LED modules.

def close_connections(self) -> None
Close all serial connections.

def init(self) -> None
Initialize the LED controller (calls setup_connections).

def close(self) -> None
Close the LED controller (calls close_connections).

def __enter__(self) -> LEDController
def __exit__(self, ...) -> None
Context manager support.

docs.title: IR Backlight
docs.text:
def initialize_IR(self, ir_intensity: int) -> None
Initialize the IR backlight at the given intensity (0–100) for all arenas.

def turn_on_backlight(self) -> None
Turn on the IR backlight for all arenas at the last set intensity.

def turn_off_backlight(self) -> None
Turn off the IR backlight for all arenas.

docs.title: Stimulus Control
docs.text:
def set_led_pulse_pattern(
    self,
    arena: int,
    pulse_width: float,
    pulse_period: float,
    pulse_count: int,
    pulse_delay: float,
    pulse_repeat: int,
    pulse_deadtime: float
) -> None
Program the pulse-train pattern for a given arena.

def set_led_stimulus_intensity(
    self,
    arena: int,
    ir: int,
    r: int,
    g: int,
    b: int
) -> None
Set the IRGB intensity (0–100) for a given arena, applying per-arena scaling factors.

def run_led_stimulus(self, arena: int) -> None
Trigger the pre-programmed stimulus on the given arena.

def reset_arena_led_stimuli(self, arena: int) -> None
Reset all LED parameters for the given arena to zero.

def reset_module_led_stimuli(self, module: int) -> None
Reset all arenas in the given LED module.

def led_stimulation(
    self,
    arena: int,
    stim_file: str
) -> None
Load a .stim JSON file and execute the full stimulation sequence on the given arena.

docs.title: Usage Example
docs.text:
from sixteeny.controller.led import LEDController

with LEDController(ports=["COM3", "COM5", "COM6", "COM4"]) as led:
    led.initialize_IR(ir_intensity=100)
    led.turn_on_backlight()

    # Deliver a stimulus on arena 0
    led.led_stimulation(arena=0, stim_file="stimulus_zoo/my_stim.stim")

    led.turn_off_backlight()

docs.title: Mass Flow Controller
docs.text:
Module: sixteeny.controller.mfc

Controls Alicat Scientific mass flow controllers (MFCs) that regulate airflow through the odor delivery system.

docs.title: Class: MFCController
docs.text: Manages one or more Alicat MFC units connected via a single serial port.

docs.title: Constructor
docs.text:
MFCController(
    com_port: str,
    device_ids: list[str],
    default_flow_rate: float
)
Parameter Type Description com_port str Serial COM port (e.g., "COM7") device_ids list[str] List of Alicat device address IDs default_flow_rate float Default flow rate in sccm applied at initialization

docs.title: Lifecycle
docs.text:
def init(self) -> None
Initialize all MFCs: test each connection and set to the default flow rate.

def close(self) -> None
Set all MFCs to zero flow and close serial connections. Retries up to 10 times on failure.

def __enter__(self) -> MFCController
def __exit__(self, ...) -> None
Context manager support.

docs.title: Flow Rate Control
docs.text:
def set_flow_rate(self, mfc_index: int, flow_rate: float) -> None
Set the flow rate for the MFC at the given index.

Parameter Type Description mfc_index int Index into device_ids list flow_rate float Target flow rate in sccm 

def get_flow_rate(self, mfc_index: int) -> float
Read the current flow rate from the MFC at the given index.

def test_connection(self, mfc_index: int) -> bool
Test whether the MFC at the given index is responsive.

docs.title: Usage Example
docs.text:
from sixteeny.controller.mfc import MFCController

with MFCController(
    com_port="COM7",
    device_ids=["A", "B"],
    default_flow_rate=100.0
) as mfc:
    # Read current flow from MFC 0
    flow = mfc.get_flow_rate(0)
    print(f"Current flow: {flow} sccm")

    # Set MFC 1 to 50 sccm
    mfc.set_flow_rate(1, 50.0)

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: Package Purpose alicat Alicat MFC Python driver

docs.title: Odor Valve Controller
docs.text:
Module: sixteeny.controller.odor

Controls the solenoid odor valves via a ROS2 interface running on a Raspberry Pi.

docs.title: Class: OdorValveController
docs.text: Publishes odor delivery commands to a ROS2 topic consumed by the Raspberry Pi valve controller.

docs.title: Constructor
docs.text:
OdorValveController(minimum_delay: float = 0.1)
Parameter Type Description minimum_delay float Minimum time in seconds between consecutive valve commands

docs.title: Lifecycle
docs.text:
def init(self, *args, **kwargs) -> None
Initialize the ROS2 node and publisher. Accepts optional rclpy.init() arguments.

def close(self) -> None
Destroy the ROS2 node and shut down the rclpy context.

def __enter__(self) -> OdorValveController
def __exit__(self, ...) -> None
Context manager support.

docs.title: Odor Delivery
docs.text:
def publish(self, arena: int, odor_vector: list[int]) -> None
Publish an odor command for a specific arena.

Parameter Type Description arena int Arena index (0–15) odor_vector list[int] Odor IDs for arms [start, left, right] — 0=air, 1=Odor 1, 2=Odor 2 

def create_msg(self, arena: int, odor_vector: list[int]) -> ArenaOdors
Create a ROS2 ArenaOdors message without publishing it.

docs.title: ROS2 Message: ArenaOdors
docs.text:
Package: y_arena_interfaces

Field Type Description arena int32 Arena index odors int32[3] Odor IDs for start, left, and right arms

docs.title: Usage Example
docs.text:
from sixteeny.controller.odor import OdorValveController

with OdorValveController(minimum_delay=0.1) as odor:
    # Set arena 0: air in start arm, Odor 1 in left, Odor 2 in right
    odor.publish(arena=0, odor_vector=[0, 1, 2])

    # Later: reset all arms to air
    odor.publish(arena=0, odor_vector=[0, 0, 0])

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: Package Purpose rclpy ROS2 Python client library y_arena_interfaces Custom ROS2 message package (must be built on both host and RPi)

docs.title: Emailer
docs.text:
Module: sixteeny.utils.emailer

Sends automated experiment progress notifications via the Gmail API.

docs.title: Class: Emailer
docs.text:
Emailer(
    config: str = "sixteeny/utils/email_config.json",
    recipients: list[str] = None,
    printer: Printer = None
)
Parameter Type Description config str Path to the email configuration JSON file recipients list[str] Override recipient email addresses (uses config file value if None) printer Printer Optional Printer instance for logging

docs.title: Configuration File Format
docs.text:
email_config.json: 

{
    "CLIENT_SECRET_FILE": "sixteeny/utils/client_secret.json",
    "SENDER_ACCOUNT": "sender@gmail.com",
    "RECIPIENT_ACCOUNTS": ["recipient1@example.com", "recipient2@example.com"]
}

docs.title: Methods
docs.text:
def get_credentials(self) -> oauth2client.Credentials
Retrieve (or refresh) OAuth2 credentials for the Gmail API. Opens a browser authentication flow on first use.

def SendMessage(
    self,
    subject: str,
    body_text: str,
    body_html: str
) -> dict
Send an email with the given subject, plain-text body, and HTML body.

Parameter Type Description subject str Email subject line body_text str Plain-text email body body_html str HTML email body

Returns the Gmail API send response dict on success, None on error.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
1. Create OAuth2 credentials in Google API Console
  2. Download as client_secret.json and place in sixteeny/utils/
  3. Run the authorization script once to generate the token: 
    test_and_authorize_email.bat

docs.title: Usage Example
docs.text:
from sixteeny.utils.emailer import Emailer

emailer = Emailer(
    config="sixteeny/utils/email_config.json",
    recipients=["pi@example.com"]
)

emailer.SendMessage(
    subject="Experiment Started",
    body_text="Experiment 'my_exp' has started with 16 arenas.",
    body_html="<b>Experiment</b> my_exp has started."
)

docs.title: Experimenter
docs.text:
Module: sixteeny.utils.experimenter

Experiment state machines that define the sequence of trials for each arena.

docs.title: Base Class: Experimenter
docs.text:
sixteeny.utils.experimenter.base

Abstract base class for all experiment types.

class Experimenter:
    def __init__(self, experiment_config_file: str)
    def get_next_trial(self, history: dict) -> dict
    def get_all_states(self) -> list[dict]

docs.title: get_next_trial(history)
docs.text:
Returns a dict describing the next trial:

{
    "reward_probability": [float, float, float],   # P(reward) per arm
    "relative_odor_vector": [int, int, int],        # odor IDs for [start, left, right]
    "time_needed_in_reward_zone": [float, ...],     # dwell time per arm (s or "inf")
    "reward_stimulus": [str, str, str],             # .stim files per arm
    "timed": bool,                                  # timed trial flag
    "odor_delay": float,                            # delay before odor delivery (s)
    "unconditioned_stimulus": str,                  # .stim file for US
    "baited": bool                                  # pre-baited trial flag
}

docs.title: Class: CSVExperimenter
docs.text:
sixteeny.utils.experimenter.openloop

Loads a trial schedule from a .csv file (used for 2AFC experiments).

CSVExperimenter(experiment_config_file: str)

The CSV must have columns: P(R|Air), P(R|O1), P(R|O2), Odor(Start), Odor(Left), Odor(Right), StayTime(Air), StayTime(O1), StayTime(O2), CStim(Air), CStim(O1), CStim(O2), Timed, OdorDelay, UStim, Baited

Supports randomized left/right assignment via 1.5 placeholders in the odor columns.

docs.title: Class: DeterministicFiniteStateExperimenter
docs.text:
sixteeny.utils.experimenter.openloop

Loads a Markov-chain DFSE experiment from a .ymaze JSON file.

DeterministicFiniteStateExperimenter(experiment_config_file: str)

Key attributes:

Attribute Description n_states Number of Markov states state_labels Reward label per state (0=unrewarded, 1=rewarded) transition_matrix (n_states, 2) — next state on Odor1/Odor2 choice current_state Current Markov state naive_trials Number of pre-task unrewarded trials

docs.title: Class: DeterministicMultilevelExperimenter
docs.text:
sixteeny.utils.experimenter.closedloop

Loads a multi-level DMLE experiment from a .ymle JSON file.

DeterministicMultilevelExperimenter(experiment_config_file: str)

Extends DeterministicFiniteStateExperimenter with three reward levels:

State Label Reward Level 0 Unrewarded 1 Low 2 Mid 3 High

Each level maps to a separate .stim file (rewarded_low_stimulus, rewarded_mid_stimulus, rewarded_high_stimulus).

docs.title: Usage Example
docs.text:
from sixteeny.utils.experimenter import (
    CSVExperimenter,
    DeterministicFiniteStateExperimenter,
    DeterministicMultilevelExperimenter
)

# 2AFC
exp = CSVExperimenter("experiment_zoo/my_2afc.csv")

# DFSE
exp = DeterministicFiniteStateExperimenter("experiment_zoo/reversal.ymaze")

# DMLE
exp = DeterministicMultilevelExperimenter("experiment_zoo/multilevel.ymle")

# Get first trial
trial = exp.get_next_trial(history={"chosen_odor": []})
print(trial["relative_odor_vector"])  # e.g. [0, 1, 2]

docs.title: Printer
docs.text:
Module: sixteeny.utils.printer

A lightweight logging utility that writes messages to both the console and a log file.

docs.title: Class: Printer
docs.text:
Printer(file_name: str, print_to_console: bool = True)
Parameter Type Description file_name str Path to the output log file print_to_console bool Whether to echo messages to stdout (default: True)

docs.title: Methods
docs.text:
def print(
    self,
    text: str,
    end: str = "\n",
    dont_print_to_console: bool = False
) -> None
Log a message. Appends to the in-memory log; optionally prints to console.

Parameter Type Description text str Message to log (converted to string) end str Line ending character (default: "\n") dont_print_to_console bool Suppress console output for this message only 

def close(self) -> None
Flush the in-memory log to the file specified at construction. Call at experiment end to persist all logs.

docs.title: Usage Example
docs.text:
from sixteeny.utils.printer import Printer

printer = Printer("experiment_log.txt", print_to_console=True)

printer.print("Experiment started")
printer.print(f"Arena 0: trial 1 started", end="\n")

# At end of experiment:
printer.close()

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Messages are buffered in memory (printer.log) and only written to disk when close() is called. This avoids frequent disk I/O during the high-speed experiment loop.
  • Progress checkpoints are printed every 100,000 lines during the file write.

docs.title: Arena Tracker
docs.text:
Module: sixteeny.utils.tracker

Tracks the state of a single fly in one Y-maze arena across an entire experiment session.

docs.title: Class: ArenaTracker
docs.text: Maintains per-arena state including fly position, current arm, trial progress, and all logged data matrices.

docs.title: Constructor
docs.text:
ArenaTracker(
    arena_index: int,
    experimenter: Experimenter,
    controllers: dict,
    printer: Printer = None
)
Parameter Type Description arena_index int Arena index (0–15) experimenter Experimenter Experiment state machine for this arena controllers dict Dict of hardware controllers ("led", "odor", "mfc") printer Printer Optional logging printer

docs.title: Key Attributes
docs.text: Attribute Type Description arena_index int This arena's index trial_count int Current trial number completed bool Whether all trials are finished fly_position np.ndarray Current [x, y] centroid current_arm int Current arm index (0=Start, 1=Left, 2=Right) in_reward_zone bool Whether fly is in the reward zone fly_positions np.ndarray (max_frames, 2) position history chosen_arms np.ndarray (n_trials,) chosen arm per trial chosen_odor np.ndarray (n_trials,) chosen odor per trial reward_delivered np.ndarray (n_trials,) reward delivery flag

docs.title: Methods
docs.text:
def relative_to_absolute_arm(
    self,
    relative_position: int,
    start_arm: int,
    arena_index: int
) -> int
Convert a relative arm position (0=Start, 1=Left, 2=Right) to an absolute arm index, accounting for clockwise/counter-clockwise arena orientation.

def update(self, fly_position: np.ndarray, frame_time: float) -> None
Process a new frame: update fly position, check arm transitions, evaluate reward zone, and trigger stimulus delivery if needed.

def start_new_trial(self) -> None
Initialize a new trial: query the experimenter for the next trial configuration, deliver odors, and reset trial state.

def end_trial(self) -> None
Finalize the current trial: log outcomes and check for experiment completion.

docs.title: Arm Coordinate System
docs.text:
Each Y-maze has 3 arms referenced in two coordinate systems:

Coordinate System Values Absolute Physical arm position 0, 1, 2 Relative Trial-start-relative 0=Start, 1=Left, 2=Right

Clockwise arenas (indices 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14) have mirrored left/right assignments compared to counter-clockwise arenas.

docs.title: Usage Example
docs.text:
from sixteeny.utils.tracker import ArenaTracker
from sixteeny.utils.experimenter import CSVExperimenter

experimenter = CSVExperimenter("experiment_zoo/my_2afc.csv")
tracker = ArenaTracker(
    arena_index=0,
    experimenter=experimenter,
    controllers={"led": led_ctrl, "odor": odor_ctrl}
)

# In the main experiment loop:
tracker.update(fly_position=np.array([320, 240]), frame_time=1.23)

docs.title: Hardware Setup
docs.text: This page describes the hardware components of the 16-Y Arena system, their connections, and configuration requirements.

docs.title: Camera — FLIR Spinnaker
docs.text:
Parameter Value SDK FLIR Spinnaker (latest) Interface USB 3.0 or GigE Driver PySpin Python API Frame rate Configurable (typically 10–30 fps) Resolution Camera-dependent

Setup notes:

  • Install the Spinnaker Full SDK from FLIR's Box
  • Install the matching Python wheel (spinnaker_python-*.whl)
  • Use FLIR SpinView to verify camera detection before running experiments

docs.title: LED Controller (Optogenetic Stimulation)
docs.text:
The system uses a custom Arduino-based LED controller with 4 modules, each controlling 4 arenas (16 total).

Parameter Value Interface Serial (RS-232 / USB-Serial) Default COM ports COM3, COM4, COM5, COM6 Baud rate 115,200 Panel IDs 0001, 0010, 1000, 0100

LED channels per arena:

  • IR — Infrared backlight for camera imaging (always on during experiment)
  • R, G, B — Visible wavelength channels for optogenetic stimulation (independently controllable)

Intensity scaling: Each channel has an independent scaling factor (0.0–1.0) applied on top of the programmed intensity to account for LED-to-LED variability.

Serial protocol: The controller accepts hex-encoded RGB intensity commands and pulse-pattern parameters.

docs.title: Odor Delivery System
docs.text:
Odor delivery is controlled via a Raspberry Pi running ROS2, which drives the solenoid odor valves.

Parameter Value Interface ROS2 over network (or USB) ROS2 topic arena_odors Message type y_arena_interfaces/msg/ArenaOdors Minimum command delay 100 ms (configurable)

Odor arms per Y-maze:

Each Y-maze has 3 arms: - Arm 0 (Start) — Air only; fly starts here at the beginning of each trial - Arm 1 (Left) — Odor 1 or Odor 2 (randomized per trial) - Arm 2 (Right) — The other odor

Odor identifiers:

ID Meaning 0 Clean air 1 Odor 1 2 Odor 2

docs.title: Mass Flow Controllers (Alicat)
docs.text:
Alicat Scientific mass flow controllers regulate the airflow through the odor delivery system.

Parameter Value Interface Serial (via COM port) Python library alicat Default flow rate Configured per experiment

Setup notes:

  • Assign unique device IDs to each MFC unit
  • Set the COM port and device IDs in the Rig Configurator
  • The system initializes each MFC to the default flow rate at startup

docs.title: Odor Maintenance
docs.text: For weekly odor vial preparation and replacement procedures, see Experiment Protocol → Y-maze Odor Replacement Instructions.

docs.title: LED Controller Serial Connections
docs.text:
Connect the four LED controller modules to USB-to-serial adapters on the Windows workstation:

Module 1 (Panel ID: 0001) → COM3
Module 2 (Panel ID: 0010) → COM5
Module 3 (Panel ID: 1000) → COM6
Module 4 (Panel ID: 0100) → COM4

COM Port Assignment

COM port numbers may differ on your machine. Use Device Manager to identify the correct COM ports and update them in the Rig Configurator.

docs.title: Arena Numbering
docs.text: The 16 arenas are arranged in a 4×4 grid. Arenas are numbered 0–15 left-to-right, top-to-bottom. Arenas at even indices (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14) are clockwise and odd arenas are counter-clockwise — this affects how left/right positions are interpreted.

docs.title: MFC Connections
docs.text: Connect the Alicat MFC to a serial port on the workstation. Set the COM port and device IDs in the Rig Configurator.

docs.title: ROS2 / Raspberry Pi Network
docs.text:
The Raspberry Pi running the odor valve controller must be reachable from the Windows workstation over the local network (or USB network adapter). Ensure:

  • Both devices are on the same network segment
  • The ROS2 domain ID matches between host and RPi
  • The y_arena_interfaces message package is installed on both machines

docs.title: Pre-experiment Hardware Checklist
docs.text:
• Camera detected by SpinView
  • All 4 LED controller serial ports visible in Device Manager
  • LED backlight powers on (test with test_loop_valves.bat)
  • MFC flow rates respond (test with MFC viewer in GUI)
  • Raspberry Pi reachable and ROS2 nodes running
  • FFMPEG accessible from terminal (ffmpeg -version)
  • Arena masks created and saved for current camera position

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: This guide covers the complete installation of the SixteenY system on a Windows workstation connected to the behavioral rig.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Before installing the software, ensure you have the following:

docs.title: Operating System
docs.text: • Windows 10 or Windows 11 (64-bit)

docs.title: Hardware Drivers & SDKs
docs.text:
• NVIDIA GPU with CUDA 11.x support (required for real-time tracking)
  • FLIR Spinnaker SDK — Full SDK + Python Spinnaker wheel (.whl)
  • FFMPEG — Added to system PATH
  • CUDA Toolkit 11.6 — Ensure CUDA_PATH environment variable is set

docs.title: Software
docs.text:
• Anaconda or Miniconda
  • Git (optional, for cloning the repository)

docs.title: 1. Create a Conda Environment
docs.text:
Open Anaconda Prompt and run:

conda create -n sixteeny python=3.8
conda activate sixteeny

Register the environment as a Jupyter kernel (optional):

conda install -c anaconda ipykernel
python -m ipykernel install --user --name=sixteeny

docs.title: 2. Install Core Python Packages
docs.text:
pip install pyserial numpy matplotlib scikit-image scikit-video PyQt5 alicat
pip install pandas seaborn

docs.title: 3. Install CUDA-Accelerated Packages
docs.text: conda install -c conda-forge cupy cudatoolkit=11.0

docs.title: 4. Install CUCIM (Windows)
docs.text:
CUCIM is used for GPU-accelerated image morphology. Install from source:

# Clone the cucim repository
git clone https://github.com/rapidsai/cucim
cd cucim/python/cucim
pip install . -v

docs.title: 5. Install the Spinnaker Python SDK
docs.text:
1. Download the Spinnaker SDK from FLIR's Box
  2. Install the Full Spinnaker SDK (the .exe installer)
  3. Unzip the Python Spinnaker SDK package
  4. Install the wheel file:
cd <download_directory>
pip install spinnaker_python-*.whl --force-reinstall --user

docs.title: 6. Install FFMPEG
docs.text:
1. Download a build from gyan.dev/ffmpeg/builds
  2. Extract to a directory (e.g., C:\ffmpeg\)
  3. Add C:\ffmpeg\bin to your system PATH

docs.title: 7. Install Google API Client (Email Notifications)
docs.text: pip install --upgrade google-api-python-client oauth2client

docs.title: 8. Configure Email Notifications
docs.text:
The system can send automated email notifications via the Gmail API.

  1. Go to the Google API Console
  2. Create an OAuth 2.0 client ID for a Desktop application
  3. Download the client_secret.json file
  4. Save it to sixteeny/utils/client_secret.json
  5. Run the email authorization script from the repository root:
python test_and_authorize_email.bat
  1. Log in to the lab Gmail account and approve the authentication flow

Credentials

Never commit your client_secret.json or OAuth token to version control.

docs.title: 9. Install the SixteenY Package
docs.text:
From the repository root directory:

pip install -e .

The -e flag installs in editable (development) mode, so changes to the source code take effect immediately.

docs.title: Setting Up Desktop Shortcuts
docs.text:
Run the provided batch script to create desktop shortcuts for all GUI applications:

initialize_shortcuts.bat

This creates shortcuts for:

  • 16Y Project Manager
  • 16Y Rig Configurator
  • 16Y Mask Designer
  • 16Y Experimenter

docs.title: Verifying the Installation
docs.text:
To confirm the installation is complete, activate the environment and check key imports:

import sixteeny
import cupy as cp
import PySpin
import PyQt5
print("Installation OK")

If all imports succeed without errors, the installation is complete.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
CuPy import fails with CUDA error

Ensure the CUDA Toolkit version matches your CuPy build. Check with: 

nvcc --version
python -c "import cupy; print(cupy.cuda.runtime.runtimeGetVersion())"
Reinstall CuPy for your specific CUDA version if needed.

PySpin import fails

Make sure you installed the Spinnaker Full SDK (not just the camera viewer) and that the Python wheel was installed with --user flag.

FFMPEG not found

Ensure C:\ffmpeg\bin (or your FFMPEG path) is added to the system PATH environment variable, not just the user PATH.

Google authentication loop

Delete the cached credentials file (~/.credentials/gmail-python-email-send.json) and re-run the authorization script.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: This guide walks you through the complete workflow for running your first SixteenY experiment — from hardware setup to data collection.

docs.title: Workflow Overview
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[1. Configure Rig] --> B[2. Design Stimuli]
    B --> C[3. Design Experiment]
    C --> D[4. Set Up Project]
    D --> E[5. Design Arena Masks]
    E --> F[6. Run Experiment]
    F --> G[7. Analyse Data]

docs.title: Step 1: Configure the Rig
docs.text:
Launch the Rig Configurator to define your hardware setup:

run_rig_configurator.bat

In the Rig Configurator, set:

  • Camera COM ports and configuration (Spinnaker)
  • LED controller serial ports (COM3, COM4, COM5, COM6) and panel IDs
  • Odor valve ROS2 connection settings
  • MFC COM port and device IDs
  • Arena-to-panel mapping

Save the configuration. This creates a rig_config.json file used by the Experimenter.

docs.title: Step 2: Design LED Stimuli
docs.text:
Launch the Stimulus Designer from the Project Manager or directly:

Define stimulus patterns with:

  • Pulse width — duration of each individual pulse (ms)
  • Pulse period — time between pulse onsets (ms)
  • Pulse count — number of pulses per stimulus
  • Pulse delay — delay before stimulus onset (ms)
  • Intensity — LED intensity (0–100%)

Save each stimulus as a .stim file in your project's stimulus_zoo/.

docs.title: Step 3: Design an Experiment
docs.text:
Launch the appropriate experiment designer based on your paradigm:

2AFCDFSEDMLE

Use the 2AFC Designer for standard reward-probability tasks:

  • Define blocks of trials with configurable numbers of trials per block
  • Set reward probability for Odor 1 and Odor 2 per block
  • Enable L/R randomization and pre-sampling options
  • Save as a .csv experiment file

Use the DFSE Designer for Deterministic Finite-State Experiments:

  • Define discrete states with reward/no-reward labels
  • Create a state-transition matrix based on the fly's choice (Odor 1 or Odor 2)
  • Set the number of naive (un-rewarded) trials before the state machine begins
  • Save as a .ymaze experiment file

Use the DMLE Designer for Multi-Level experiments:

  • Extends DFSE with three reward levels: low, mid, and high
  • Assign different stimuli to each reward level
  • Save as a .ymle experiment file

docs.title: Step 4: Set Up a Project
docs.text:
Launch the Project Manager:

run_project_manager.bat

A project directory has the following structure:

my_experiment/
├── data/                  # Experiment output data
├── stimulus_zoo/          # .stim files
├── experiment_zoo/        # .csv / .ymaze / .ymle files
└── genotypes.log          # Fly genotype registry

Use the Project Manager to: - Browse and organize experiments and stimuli - Launch experiment and stimulus designers - Trigger post-hoc data processing

docs.title: Step 5: Design Arena Masks
docs.text:
Before running experiments, you must create an arena mask — a set of region-of-interest polygons that map pixel coordinates to Y-maze arms for all 16 arenas.

Launch the Mask Designer:

run_mask_designer.bat
  1. Load a background frame captured from your camera
  2. Enter the number of Y-arenas (16)
  3. Click the center and arm tips of each arena to define keypoints
  4. The tool automatically generates a mask and saves it as a .npy file

docs.title: Step 6: Run an Experiment
docs.text:
Launch the Experimenter:

run_experiment.bat

Configure the session:

  1. Select your project directory
  2. Enter an experiment name (optionally append a timestamp)
  3. Select the fly genotype for each of the 16 arenas
  4. Set the starvation start time for each arena
  5. Select the experiment file (.csv, .ymaze, or .ymle) for each arena
  6. Adjust hardware settings (camera exposure, LED scaling, MFC flow rates)
  7. Set the arena mask file

Click Start Experiment to begin. The Experimenter will:

  • Initialize all hardware controllers
  • Capture a background frame
  • Begin the tracking loop (GPU-accelerated)
  • Deliver odor and LED stimuli according to each fly's choices
  • Log all data to disk in real time
  • Send email notifications at key milestones

docs.title: Step 7: Analyse Data
docs.text:
After the experiment, use the Data Processor from the Project Manager to:

  • Align and clean trajectory data
  • Compute choice fractions, response times, and reward rates
  • Generate summary plots
  • Export processed data to CSV

Use the Video Generator to create annotated video overlays of fly trajectories for visual inspection.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Hardware Setup — detailed rig wiring and hardware configuration
  • Experiment Designers — in-depth guide to each paradigm
  • API Reference — Python API for custom scripting

docs.title: Analysis Tools
docs.text: SixteenY includes built-in post-hoc analysis and visualization tools that are launched from the Project Manager.

docs.title: Data Processor
docs.text:
The 16Y Data Processor GUI for post-hoc analysis of completed experiments.

The Data Processor (16Y_data_processor.py) provides a GUI for loading, cleaning, and analyzing data from completed experiments.

docs.title: Launching
docs.text: Select one or more completed experiments in the Project Manager's Experimental Data panel, then click Process Data.

docs.title: 1. Load Raw Data
docs.text: The Data Processor reads per-arena tracking arrays (tracking.npy) and experiment logs (experiment_log.json) from each arena folder.

docs.title: 2. Trajectory Alignment
docs.text:
Fly position trajectories are aligned into a canonical coordinate frame:

  • L/R Alignment — Positions are rotated so that the start arm is always at the bottom, left arm on the left, and right arm on the right.
  • Odor Alignment — Positions can be re-aligned so that Odor 1 is always on the left, accounting for L/R randomization.
# Left-Start-Right coordinate realignment
realign_lr(start_arm, point, arena_index)

# Odor 1-Start-Odor 2 coordinate realignment
realign_odor(odor_vector, point)

docs.title: 3. Computed Metrics
docs.text:
Per trial and per session, the processor computes:

Metric Description Choice Fraction Fraction of trials where Odor 1 (or left) was chosen Response Time Time from trial start to arm entry Reward Rate Fraction of trials where reward was delivered Dwell Time Time spent in the chosen arm Trial Length Total duration of each trial

docs.title: 4. Export
docs.text: Processed data is exported to CSV files in the experiment folder for downstream analysis (e.g., in Jupyter notebooks or R).

docs.title: Video Generator
docs.text: The Video Generator (16Y_video_generator.py) creates annotated trajectory video overlays.

docs.title: Launching
docs.text: Select experiments in the Project Manager and click Generate Video.

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
For each experiment, the Video Generator produces an .mp4 video showing:

  • Raw camera frames (or a background image)
  • Fly trajectory overlay per arena
  • Arena mask overlay (arm boundaries)
  • Trial and choice annotations

Videos are saved to the experiment folder using FFMPEG.

docs.title: Manual Analysis
docs.text: For custom analysis, the raw data files are directly accessible:

docs.title: Tracking Array Schema
docs.text:
Load with NumPy:

import numpy as np
data = np.load("arena_0/tracking.npy", allow_pickle=True).item()

# Available fields:
data["fly_positions"]               # (N, 2) - x/y pixel positions
data["frame_times"]                 # (N,)   - timestamps
data["current_arms"]                # (N,)   - arm index per frame
data["current_trials"]              # (N,)   - trial index per frame
data["current_reward_zone_status"]  # (N,)   - in reward zone (bool)
data["trial_start_times"]           # (T,)   - trial start timestamps
data["trial_end_times"]             # (T,)   - trial end timestamps
data["chosen_arms"]                 # (T,)   - chosen arm per trial
data["chosen_odor"]                 # (T,)   - chosen odor per trial
data["reward_delivered"]            # (T,)   - reward delivered (bool)
data["odor_vectors"]                # (T, 3) - odor arm mapping per trial

docs.title: Experiment Log
docs.text:
The experiment_log.json contains the full trial-by-trial state history, including reward probabilities, stimulus assignments, and experimenter state.

import json
with open("experiment_log.json") as f:
    log = json.load(f)
# log["states"] is a list of dicts, one per trial

docs.title: Experiment Designers
docs.text: SixteenY includes three experiment design tools, each implementing a different behavioral paradigm. All designers save experiment files to the project's experiment_zoo/ directory.

docs.title: 2AFC Designer
docs.text: The 2AFC Designer creates standard Two-Alternative Forced Choice experiments where flies must choose between two odors with configurable reward probabilities.

docs.title: Screenshot
docs.text: The 2AFC Designer: each row is one block of trials with configurable trial count and reward probabilities for each odor.

docs.title: Launching
docs.text:
From the Project Manager, click New 2AFC Experiment, or run:

# From the command line
python -m sixteeny.gui.16Y_2AFC_designer

docs.title: Design Parameters
docs.text:
A 2AFC experiment is organized into blocks of trials, each with:

Parameter Description Number of Trials How many trials in this block Reward Probability (Odor 1) P(reward | fly chooses Odor 1 arm), 0.0–1.0 Reward Probability (Odor 2) P(reward | fly chooses Odor 2 arm), 0.0–1.0

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description Randomize L/R Randomly assign Odor 1 and Odor 2 to left/right arm each trial Presampled L/R Pre-generate L/R assignments (reproducible randomness) Expectation Sampling Oversample L/R assignments to enforce exact empirical ratios Odor Delay Delay (seconds) between fly entering arm and odor delivery Dwell Time Minimum time fly must remain in arm before reward is evaluated Timed Trials If enabled, trials end after a fixed duration regardless of fly choice Baited Trials Whether the reward arm is pre-baited at trial start Stimulus Files Assign .stim LED patterns for rewarded and unrewarded arms Unconditioned Stimulus Optional additional stimulus delivered independently of choice

docs.title: Output Format
docs.text:
Saves as a .csv file with one row per trial:

P(R|Air),P(R|O1),P(R|O2),Odor(Start),Odor(Left),Odor(Right),
StayTime(Air),StayTime(O1),StayTime(O2),CStim(Air),CStim(O1),CStim(O2),
Timed,OdorDelay,UStim,Baited

docs.title: DFSE Designer (Deterministic Finite-State Experiment)
docs.text: The DFSE Designer implements a reversal-learning / contingency task where reward availability is governed by a Markov chain that transitions based on the fly's choices.

docs.title: Launching
docs.text: From the Project Manager, click New DFSE Experiment.

docs.title: Design Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description Number of States Total states in the state machine State Labels Reward level per state: 0 = unrewarded, 1 = rewarded Transition Matrix [n_states × 2] — T[i, 0] = next state if fly chooses Odor 1; T[i, 1] = next state if fly chooses Odor 2 Naive Trials Number of initial trials with no reward (state machine inactive) Total Trials Total number of trials (naive + task) Rewarded Stimulus .stim file used when reward is given Unrewarded Stimulus .stim file used when reward is withheld L/R Randomization Whether to randomize left/right assignment

docs.title: Example: Simple Reversal
docs.text:
A simple 2-state reversal (Odor 1 rewarded → Odor 2 rewarded):

States:      S0 (Odor 1 rewarded)    S1 (Odor 2 rewarded)
Transitions: S0 →[O1]→ S0            S0 →[O2]→ S1
             S1 →[O1]→ S0            S1 →[O2]→ S1

docs.title: Output Format
docs.text: Saves as a .ymaze JSON file containing state labels, transition matrix, stimulus assignments, and trial counts.

docs.title: DMLE Designer (Deterministic Multi-Level Experiment)
docs.text: The DMLE Designer extends DFSE with three reward levels — low, mid, and high — enabling experiments that test graded value representations and multi-level contingency learning.

docs.title: Launching
docs.text: From the Project Manager, click New DMLE Experiment.

docs.title: Design Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description Number of States Total states in the state machine State Labels Reward level per state: 0 = unrewarded, 1 = low, 2 = mid, 3 = high Transition Matrix Same as DFSE: [n_states × 2] Naive Trials Number of unrewarded naive trials Total Trials Total number of trials Rewarded Low/Mid/High Stimuli Three .stim files for each reward magnitude Unrewarded Stimulus .stim file for unrewarded trials L/R Randomization Whether to randomize left/right assignment

docs.title: Output Format
docs.text: Saves as a .ymle JSON file, structurally similar to .ymaze but with additional reward-level fields.

docs.title: Choosing a Paradigm
docs.text: Paradigm Use When 2AFC Fixed reward probabilities; studying odor preference or bias DFSE Reversal learning, contingency tasks, reward tracking DMLE Value-based choice, multi-level reward coding

docs.title: Experiment Protocol
docs.text:
This page describes the standard operating procedures for running Y-maze behavioral experiments, maintaining the odor delivery system, and preparing flies. These instructions were developed by the Turner Lab at Janelia Research Campus.

Site-Specific Values (Turner Lab, Janelia Research Campus)

The table below lists the location and path values used by the Turner Lab. Replace these with the values appropriate for your own setup wherever you see the corresponding placeholder in this document.

Placeholder Turner Lab value <project_directory> D:\AparnaAlt\<current_project> <software_directory> C:\16YArena\TurnerLab_Opto2AFC_16Y\ <rig_config_file> D:\AparnaAlt\config.yarena <fly_incubator> Incubator E (21 °C, 9 am–1 am light cycle), near FlyCore <tray_label> "Turner 16Y" <ymaze_room> Room 2E.361.4 <prep_bench> Bench at 2E.235.4 (Turner lab) <vial_storage> Box above bench at 2E.235.3 <fume_hood_room> Shared weigh room, 2E.248 <supplies_room> LSR, 2E.252

docs.title: Weekly Schedule at a Glance
docs.text: Day Fly starvation Experiments Odor maintenance Monday Starve flies (for Wednesday runs) — — Tuesday Starve flies (for Thursday runs) — Replace odor vials Wednesday Starve flies (for Friday runs) Morning + Afternoon — Thursday — Morning + Afternoon — Friday — Morning + Afternoon —

docs.title: Y-maze Assay Instructions
docs.text: Authors: Claire Manangan — Turner Lab, Janelia Research Campus (October 2024)

docs.title: Schedule
docs.text: Experiments are run in the morning and afternoon on Wednesday, Thursday, and Friday.

docs.title: Starvation Preparation
docs.text:
Flies must be starved for 40–46 hours before an experiment (typically 40–42 h for morning sessions, 44–46 h for afternoon sessions).

Transfer adult flies into starvation vials:

  1. Prepare vials containing 2% agar with approximately 0.5 cm of agar at the bottom of a standard fly vial. The agar provides hydration but no nutrition.
  2. Transfer the appropriate flies into the starvation vials and plug them.
  3. Label each vial with the genotype and the exact date and time of transfer.
  4. Return the vials to <fly_incubator>.

Starvation timing

For the standard Wed/Thu/Fri schedule, flies are moved to starvation vials on Monday, Tuesday, and Wednesday respectively — 2 days before their experiment day.

docs.title: Fly Preparation
docs.text:
1. Retrieve the tray of flies for that day's experiment from <fly_incubator>.
  2. The trays should be labeled <tray_label>.
  3. Look for the tray with a starvation date 2 days before the current date:
    • Flies run on Wednesday are starved on Monday
    • Flies run on Thursday are starved on Tuesday
    • Flies run on Friday are starved on Wednesday
  4. Take the flies to <ymaze_room>.

docs.title: Setting Up the Assay
docs.text:
1. Open the door to the rig and loosen the screws if needed.
  2. Make sure the plates covering each Y-maze are positioned so the hole is above one of the arms.
  3. Load one fly into each Y-maze using a mouth pipette, excluding maze 0 and maze 3.
  4. Each tray should have approximately 40 control flies and 40 experimental flies.
  5. If both control and experimental flies are being run, alternate which group goes first on each subsequent day.
  6. Once a fly is in the maze, use the tip of the mouth pipette to slide the plate to the closed position.
  7. Once all flies are loaded, tighten the screws and close the door.
  8. Run the validator.bat script:
    • The script is in <project_directory>.
    • It checks which experiments were run last and records the next experiments in next_to_run.log.
    • Press Enter four times to accept the default settings:
      • "Enter number of replicates per experiment (leave empty for default = 1):" → Enter
      • "Enter minimum number of trials per experiment (leave empty for default = 180):" → Enter
      • "Enter minimum number of experiments per run (leave empty for default = 15):" → Enter
      • "Clear cache? (y/n) (default = n):" → Enter
    • Once finished, press any key to close the Command Prompt window.
  9. Execute the run_experiment.bat script from <software_directory>. This opens a Command Prompt window and then the 16-Y Experimenter GUI.
  10. In the 16-Y Experimenter GUI:
    • Click Browse next to Project Directory and navigate to <project_directory>.
    • For Experiment Name, calculate starvation duration from the start time written on the tray. Use the format:
      • Xhr_starvation_EXP for experimental flies
      • Xhr_starvation_CONT for control flies
    • Click Add Timestamp to append the current date and time.
    • For Fly Genotype, select the correct genotype from the dropdown. If not listed, choose custom and type the genotype name.
    • For Starvation Start, enter the starvation time calculated above and click Update to autofill the date/time. Edit to the closest half-hour if needed.
    • Click Toggle All Arenas, then uncheck Fly 0 and Fly 3.
    • Click Copy Genotypes, Copy Starvation Starts, and Copy Comments.
    • Click Auto-Populate Experiments from File and select next_to_run.log from <project_directory>.
    • Click Start Experiment.
  11. When the Create Rig Configuration File pop-up appears, click Yes to open the Rig Configurator GUI:
    • Click Browse next to Experiment Directory and navigate to the data folder inside <project_directory>. Select the folder with the correct date/timestamp.
    • Click Load Configuration and select <rig_config_file>.
    • Click No on the Change Experiment Directory pop-up.
    • Click Save Configuration, then OK on the Configuration Saved pop-up.
    • Click Yes on the Save Configuration pop-up to close the Rig Configurator.
  12. Click OK on the Rig Configuration File Present window to begin running.
  13. Open the two air supply valves and flip the switch on the black power unit (plugged into the surge protector) to turn on the MFCs (mass flow controllers).
  14. Answer the questions in the Command Prompt window:
    • "Have you turned on the MFCs and air supply? (y/n)" → y
  15. A background image will open in Figure 1. Check for any flies visible in the background:
    • No flies visible → "Is this the background image? (y/n)" → y
    • Flies visible → "Is this the background image? (y/n)" → n, then "Background image capture failed. Retry? (y/n)" → y
  16. Once confirmed, a mask file will open in Figure 1. Verify no flies are visible in the mask, close the window, then:
    • "Is this the mask? (y/n)" → y
    • "Start in debug mode? (y/n)" → y
  17. Experiments will now run and finish in approximately 2.5 hours.

docs.title: Shutting Down and Resetting the Assay
docs.text:
1. Close both air supply valves and turn off the MFCs.
  2. In the Command Prompt window: "Did you turn off the air supply and MFCs? (y/n)" → y
  3. Close the Command Prompt window (this automatically closes the 16-Y Experimenter GUI).
  4. Open the door to the rig and loosen all the screws.
  5. Use a mouth pipette to remove the flies from each Y-maze:
    • Slide the plate covering the maze to the open position and suck up the fly.
  6. Dispose of the flies in the fly morgue.
  7. To run another experiment, repeat Setting Up the Assay steps 1–10.
  8. After the final experiment of the day, repeat steps 1–4 above.

docs.title: Y-maze Odor Replacement Instructions
docs.text:
Authors: Claire Manangan — Turner Lab, Janelia Research Campus (October 2024)

Odor vials are replaced each Tuesday.

Chemical Safety

Always prepare and handle odors (MHO, HAL) inside a fume hood. Wear gloves at all times when handling neat odors or odor solutions. Dispose of all odor waste — including old vial contents — in the designated liquid waste container. Never pour odor solutions down the drain.

docs.title: Preparing New Odor Vials
docs.text:
1. Locate the two empty blue racks in <ymaze_room>:
    • One rack labeled "MHO 1:1000" (green tape)
    • One rack labeled "HAL 1:1000" (orange tape)
  2. Take the racks to <prep_bench> and fill each with 16 new empty amber vials.
    • Amber vials are stored at <vial_storage>.
  3. Get two 250 mL jars from the glass supply closet and fill each with 150 mL of Paraffin oil.
    • Label one jar "MHO" (green tape) and the other "HAL" (orange tape).
  4. Take the jars to the fume hood in <fume_hood_room>.
  5. Put on gloves before handling odors. Odors are stored in the yellow flammables cabinet opposite the fume hood:
    • One bottle labeled "MHO", one labeled "HAL".
  6. In the fume hood, add 15 µL (1:1000) of each odor to its corresponding jar.
  7. Add a small stir bar to each jar and spin for a few minutes at full power.
  8. Obtain two 10 mL auto-pipette tips from <supplies_room>. Fill each amber vial with 7.5 mL of the corresponding odor solution.
  9. Pour excess odor solution into the liquid waste container.
  10. Rinse the jars and place them in the glass cleaning trays.

docs.title: Replacing Odor Vials on the Rig
docs.text:
1. Take the newly filled racks to <ymaze_room>.
  2. Remove the old vials by twisting them clockwise.
    • Black rubber gaskets and thin plastic tubes may fall off — set gaskets aside and use the handheld mirror to replace any plastic tubes.
  3. Attach the new vials by twisting them counterclockwise.
  4. The rig has two "sets" of odor vials, each with three rows:
    • The inner row (closest to the middle) contains no odors — do not remove these.
    • The middle row of each set → Odor 1 (MHO)
    • The outer row (closest to the edge) → Odor 2 (HAL)
    • Viewed from the front: Odor 2 – Odor 1 – Air :: Air – Odor 1 – Odor 2
  5. Check each vial position for a black rubber gasket using the handheld mirror. Place a gasket on top of any new vial before screwing it in if one is missing.
  6. Periodically check the water level in the jars below the odor vials. Fill with DI water from a nearby sink (water should last a few weeks).
  7. Return the old vials to the now-empty racks and take them to <prep_bench>.
  8. Put on gloves and pour the old vial contents into a beaker, then empty into the liquid waste container.
  9. Rinse the beaker and place it in the glass cleaning tray.
  10. Dispose of old amber vials in the large sharps container.
    • If the container is full, obtain a replacement from <supplies_room>.
  11. Return the empty racks to <ymaze_room>.

docs.title: Experimenter
docs.text: The 16Y Experimenter is the main application for running live behavioral experiments across all 16 arenas simultaneously.

docs.title: Launching
docs.text: run_experiment.bat

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The 16Y Experimenter GUI: assign genotypes and experiment files to each of the 16 arenas before starting a session.

docs.title: Pre-experiment Setup
docs.text: Before clicking Start, complete all sections of the Experimenter interface:

docs.title: 1. Project Directory
docs.text: Click Browse to select your project folder. The Experimenter will load genotype history and look for experiment/stimulus files from this directory.

docs.title: 2. Experiment Name
docs.text: Enter a descriptive name for the session. Click Add Timestamp to automatically append the current date and time — useful for keeping sessions uniquely identifiable.

docs.title: 3. Fly Genotypes
docs.text:
For each of the 16 arenas, select or type the fly genotype. Use Copy Genotypes to apply the same genotype to all arenas at once.

Previously used genotypes from genotypes.log appear in the dropdown.

docs.title: 4. Starvation Start Time
docs.text:
Enter the date and time when each fly (or cohort) was moved to starvation conditions. This is recorded in the metadata for downstream analysis.

Use Quick Buttons (e.g., "−16 h", "−24 h") to set relative starvation times quickly.

docs.title: 5. Experiment File
docs.text: For each arena, select the experiment file (.csv, .ymaze, or .ymle) to run. Use the Copy button to apply the same file to all arenas.

docs.title: 6. Camera and Hardware Settings
docs.text: Setting Description Mask File Path to the .npy arena mask Camera Index Which Spinnaker camera to use Exposure / Gain Camera acquisition parameters GPU Enabled Toggle GPU-accelerated tracking (recommended) LED Scaling Fine-tune per-arena LED intensity MFC Flow Rate Airflow rate override Email Notifications Toggle experiment progress emails Reminder Interval Frequency of email reminders after experiment end

docs.title: Running an Experiment
docs.text: Click Start Experiment when all settings are configured.

docs.title: Initialization Sequence
docs.text:
1. Hardware controllers are initialized (camera, LEDs, valves, MFCs)
  2. IR backlights are turned on
  3. A background frame is captured and saved
  4. The GPU tracking pipeline is initialized

docs.title: Live Experiment Loop
docs.text:
For each camera frame:

  1. Frame is acquired from the Spinnaker camera
  2. GPU-accelerated change detection identifies fly positions
  3. Each arena's ArenaTracker processes the fly position:
  4. Determines which arm the fly is in
  5. Detects reward-zone entries
  6. Queries the experimenter for the next trial state
  7. Issues odor and LED stimulus commands as appropriate
  8. Frame and tracking data are saved asynchronously to disk

docs.title: Trial Logic
docs.text:
Each trial follows this sequence:

Fly enters arm
    ↓
Odor delivered (after optional odor delay)
    ↓
Fly stays in arm for required dwell time
    ↓
Reward evaluated (probabilistic)
    ↓
LED stimulus delivered (if reward)
    ↓
Trial logged, next trial loaded
    ↓
Fly returns to start arm → new trial begins

docs.title: Stopping the Experiment
docs.text:
Click Stop Experiment to gracefully end the session. The system will:

  1. Complete any in-progress trials
  2. Turn off all stimuli
  3. Close hardware connections
  4. Save all tracking data
  5. Write experiment logs
  6. Send a completion email notification

docs.title: Data Output
docs.text:
All data is saved to <project_dir>/data/<experiment_name>/:

experiment_name/
├── background.png             # Background frame used for tracking
├── experiment_config.json     # Full experiment configuration snapshot
├── arena_0/
│   ├── tracking.npy           # Position, arm, trial, reward data
│   ├── experiment_log.json    # Trial-by-trial state log
│   └── frames/                # Raw camera frames (if enabled)
├── arena_1/
│   └── ...
└── ...

docs.title: Tracking Data Format (tracking.npy)
docs.text:
The tracking array contains per-frame columns:

Column Description fly_x, fly_y Fly centroid position in pixels current_arm Current arm index (0=Start, 1=Left, 2=Right) current_trial Trial number in_reward_zone Boolean reward zone status frame_time Timestamp (seconds)

docs.title: Email Notifications
docs.text:
If email is enabled, the system sends notifications at:

  • Experiment start
  • Every N trials completed (configurable)
  • Experiment end / all arenas complete
  • Periodic reminders if valves are left on after completion

Configure the Gmail API credentials as described in Installation.

docs.title: Lab Protocol
docs.text: For step-by-step instructions on loading flies, running sessions, and weekly odor replacement, see the Experiment Protocol.

docs.title: Mask Designer
docs.text: The 16Y Mask Designer is a graphical tool for creating arena masks — pixel-coordinate maps that tell the tracking system where each Y-maze arm is in the camera image.

docs.title: Launching
docs.text: run_mask_designer.bat

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Mask Designer's labelling interface showing all 16 arenas with numbered keypoints (center, start-arm tip, left-arm tip, right-arm tip) overlaid on the camera image.

docs.title: What is an Arena Mask?
docs.text:
An arena mask is a NumPy array (.npy) that encodes, for every pixel in the camera image, which Y-maze arena and which arm (0=Start, 1=Left, 2=Right, or background) that pixel belongs to.

The tracker uses this mask to:

  1. Determine which arm a fly is currently in
  2. Detect when a fly enters the reward zone (arm tip)
  3. Compute choice (left vs. right arm selection)

docs.title: Step 1: Load a Background Image
docs.text: Click Get Background Image and select a background frame captured from the camera (e.g., a .png exported during rig setup or from a test capture). The image will be displayed in the main viewer.

docs.title: Step 2: Set Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description Number of Y-Arenas Total arenas to label (typically 16) Choice Boundary Distance Distance from arena center to the choice boundary (pixels); defines when a fly has committed to an arm

docs.title: Step 3: Label the Arenas
docs.text:
Click Start Labelling to begin the point-clicking interface.

For each arena, click the following keypoints in order:

  1. Center — The junction point of the Y
  2. Start arm tip — The tip of the start (bottom) arm
  3. Left arm tip — The tip of the left arm
  4. Right arm tip — The tip of the right arm

The tool draws the polygon mask and displays the labeled regions in real time.

Labelling Order

Label arenas systematically (e.g., left-to-right, top-to-bottom) to ensure the arena indices match the LED controller and valve controller numbering.

docs.title: Step 4: Review and Save
docs.text:
After labelling all arenas:

  1. Click Plot Mask to visualize the complete mask overlaid on the background image
  2. Click Save Mask to export the mask as a .npy file

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Camera must be fixed — The mask is specific to the exact camera position and zoom. If the rig is moved, a new mask must be created.
  • Consistent keypoint order — The tool assumes Start → Left → Right arm tip order.
  • Re-use masks — As long as the rig geometry hasn't changed, the same mask can be re-used across all experiments.

docs.title: Output Format
docs.text:
The saved .npy mask is a 2D integer array with the same shape as the camera image:

Value Meaning -1 Background (outside all arenas) 3*i + 0 Arena i, Start arm 3*i + 1 Arena i, Left arm 3*i + 2 Arena i, Right arm

where i ranges from 0 to 15.

docs.title: User Guide Overview
docs.text: The 16FlYMaze system is operated through a suite of seven graphical applications, each designed for a specific stage of the experimental workflow.

docs.title: Application Suite
docs.text:
graph TD
    A[🧫 Project Manager] -->|organize experiments| B[📐 Experiment Designers]
    A -->|organize stimuli| C[💡 Stimulus Designer]
    A -->|launch| D[🔬 Experimenter]
    E[⚙️ Rig Configurator] -->|hardware config| D
    F[🗺️ Mask Designer] -->|arena masks| D
    B -->|experiment files| D
    C -->|stimulus files| D
    D -->|raw data| G[📊 Analysis Tools]

docs.title: Applications at a Glance
docs.text: Application Launcher Purpose Project Manager run_project_manager.bat Organize data, experiments, and stimuli Rig Configurator run_rig_configurator.bat Configure hardware COM ports and settings Mask Designer run_mask_designer.bat Draw Y-arena ROI masks from camera images Experimenter run_experiment.bat Run live experiments with real-time tracking Experiment Designers Launched from Project Manager Design 2AFC, DFSE, and DMLE experiments Stimulus Designer Launched from Project Manager Create LED pulse-pattern stimulus files Analysis Tools Launched from Project Manager Post-hoc data processing and visualization

docs.title: Typical Session Workflow
docs.text:
A typical experimental session follows this order:

  1. Configure the rig (once, or after hardware changes) → Rig Configurator

  2. Design stimuli (once per stimulus type) → Stimulus Designer

  3. Design experiments (once per paradigm) → Experiment Designers

  4. Set up a project (once per experimental cohort) → Project Manager

  5. Create arena masks (once, or after rig repositioning) → Mask Designer

  6. Run experiments (each session) → Experimenter

  7. Analyse data (after each session or batch) → Analysis Tools

For day-to-day operational procedures — fly starvation, loading, and odor replacement — refer to the Experiment Protocol.

docs.title: File Types
docs.text: Extension Type Created by .stim LED stimulus pattern (JSON) Stimulus Designer .csv 2AFC experiment schedule 2AFC Designer .ymaze DFSE experiment config (JSON) DFSE Designer .ymle DMLE experiment config (JSON) DMLE Designer .npy Arena mask array Mask Designer rig_config.json Hardware configuration Rig Configurator

docs.title: Project Manager
docs.text: The 16Y Project Manager is the central hub for organizing all files associated with your experiments.

docs.title: Launching
docs.text:
run_project_manager.bat

Or double-click the 16Y Project Manager desktop shortcut (after running initialize_shortcuts.bat).

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The 16Y Project Manager showing the Experimental Data, Stimulus Zoo, and Experiment Zoo panels.

The Project Manager displays three panels side-by-side:

Panel Contents Experimental Data Completed experiment folders in data/ Stimulus Zoo Available .stim files in stimulus_zoo/ Experiment Zoo Available experiment files (.csv, .ymaze, .ymle) in experiment_zoo/

docs.title: Project Directory Structure
docs.text:
A SixteenY project follows this directory structure:

my_project/
├── data/                     # Completed experiment outputs
│   └── experiment_name/
│       ├── arena_0/          # Per-arena data
│       │   ├── tracking.npy
│       │   └── experiment_log.json
│       ├── ...
│       ├── background.png
│       └── experiment_config.json
├── stimulus_zoo/             # Stimulus files
│   ├── my_stimulus.stim
│   └── ...
├── experiment_zoo/           # Experiment design files
│   ├── my_2afc.csv
│   ├── my_dfse.ymaze
│   └── ...
└── genotypes.log             # Registry of fly genotypes used

docs.title: Opening a Project
docs.text:
1. Click Browse to select an existing project directory
  2. Click Create New to create a new project folder

The three panels automatically populate with files found in the project directory.

docs.title: Launching Designers
docs.text:
• New Stimulus — Opens the Stimulus Designer pre-configured for the current project
  • New Experiment — Opens the appropriate experiment designer
  • Edit Stimulus / Experiment — Opens the selected file in its designer

docs.title: Processing Experimental Data
docs.text: Select one or more completed experiment folders in the Experimental Data panel, then click Process Data to launch the Data Processor for batch post-hoc analysis.

docs.title: Generating Videos
docs.text: Select experiment folders and click Generate Video to create annotated trajectory video overlays.

docs.title: Genotypes Log
docs.text: The genotypes.log file tracks fly genotypes used across all experiments in the project. The Experimenter reads this file to populate the genotype dropdown with previously used genotype strings, ensuring consistency.

docs.title: Rig Configurator
docs.text: The 16Y Rig Configurator allows you to define and save all hardware settings for your specific rig setup.

docs.title: Launching
docs.text: run_rig_configurator.bat

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The 16Y Rig Configurator GUI showing LED, camera, MFC, and odor valve settings.

The Rig Configurator stores all hardware configuration in a rig_config.json file that is loaded by the Experimenter at startup. You only need to reconfigure when hardware connections change.

docs.title: Camera Settings
docs.text: Parameter Description Camera Index Index of the Spinnaker camera to use (0 for first camera) Exposure Time Camera exposure time in microseconds Gain Camera analog gain Frame Rate Target acquisition frame rate (fps) Image Width / Height Camera resolution in pixels Pixel Format e.g., Mono8 for 8-bit grayscale

docs.title: LED Controller Settings
docs.text: Parameter Description COM Ports List of 4 COM ports for the LED modules (e.g., COM3, COM5, COM6, COM4) Baud Rate Serial baud rate (default: 115,200) Panel IDs 4-character binary IDs identifying each module (0001, 0010, 1000, 0100) IR/R/G/B Scaling Factors Per-channel intensity scaling (0.0–1.0) for each of the 16 arenas IR Intensity Default IR backlight intensity (0–100%)

docs.title: Odor Valve Settings
docs.text: Parameter Description Minimum Delay Minimum time (seconds) between valve commands (default: 0.1) ROS2 Configuration Network/domain settings for the Raspberry Pi interface

docs.title: MFC Settings
docs.text: Parameter Description COM Port Serial port for the Alicat MFC Device IDs List of MFC device address IDs Default Flow Rate Default flow rate (sccm) applied at initialization

docs.title: Arena Layout
docs.text: Parameter Description Arena Panel IDs Mapping from arena index (0–15) to LED panel module Clockwise Arenas List of arena indices with clockwise orientation (default: even indices)

docs.title: Saving and Loading
docs.text:
• Click Save Configuration to write the current settings to rig_config.json
  • The Experimenter automatically loads this file at startup
  • Use Load Configuration to restore a previously saved config
  • Use Reset to Defaults to restore factory default values

docs.title: Testing Hardware
docs.text:
The Rig Configurator includes hardware test buttons:

Button Action Test Camera Capture and display a test frame Test LEDs Flash each LED module in sequence Test Valves Cycle through odor valve states Test MFCs Read current flow rates from all MFCs

docs.title: Stimulus Designer
docs.text: The Stimulus Designer creates LED pulse-pattern stimulus files (.stim) that define the timing and intensity of optogenetic light delivery.

docs.title: Launching
docs.text:
From the Project Manager, click New Stimulus, or double-click an existing .stim file to edit it.

The 16Y Stimulus Designer showing a 500 ms red-light pulse with live waveform preview.

docs.title: Stimulus Parameters
docs.text:
A stimulus is defined by a train of rectangular pulses:

│←delay→│←width→│←─── period ───→│←width→│...
        ▄▄▄▄▄▄▄                  ▄▄▄▄▄▄▄
        │       │                │       │
────────┘       └────────────────┘       └────
Parameter Description Units Pulse Width Duration of each individual pulse milliseconds Pulse Period Time from the start of one pulse to the start of the next milliseconds Pulse Count Number of pulses in the train — Pulse Delay Delay before the first pulse begins milliseconds Pulse Repeat Number of times the entire pulse train repeats — Dead Time Gap between repeats of the pulse train milliseconds

docs.title: Intensity Settings
docs.text:
Each of the four LED channels (IR, R, G, B) can be independently configured:

Parameter Description IR Intensity Intensity of the infrared backlight channel (0–100%) R Intensity Intensity of the red channel (0–100%) G Intensity Intensity of the green channel (0–100%) B Intensity Intensity of the blue channel (0–100%)

docs.title: Waveform Preview
docs.text: The Stimulus Designer renders a live waveform preview of the stimulus in a matplotlib figure as you adjust parameters. This lets you visually verify the pulse train before saving.

docs.title: Saving a Stimulus
docs.text: Click Save to write the stimulus to a .stim file (JSON format) in the project's stimulus_zoo/ directory.

docs.title: File Format
docs.text:
{
  "pulse_width": 10,
  "pulse_period": 50,
  "pulse_count": 10,
  "pulse_delay": 0,
  "pulse_repeat": 1,
  "pulse_deadtime": 0,
  "ir_intensity": 100,
  "r_intensity": 0,
  "g_intensity": 80,
  "b_intensity": 0
}

docs.title: Using Stimuli in Experiments
docs.text:
When designing an experiment (2AFC, DFSE, or DMLE), assign .stim files to each outcome:

  • Conditioned Stimulus — Delivered when the fly makes a specific choice (per arm)
  • Unconditioned Stimulus — Delivered independently of choice (e.g., as an US in classical conditioning)

The Experimenter loads and parses .stim files at runtime and sends the corresponding commands to the LED controller via serial.

docs.title: Constant Light (Tonic)
docs.text: • Pulse width = 1000 ms, Period = 1001 ms, Count = 1

docs.title: Pulsed (10 Hz)
docs.text: • Pulse width = 50 ms, Period = 100 ms, Count = 10

docs.title: Ramp (via repeats)
docs.text: • Use multiple stimuli with increasing intensity, triggered sequentially via the unconditioned stimulus mechanism
